posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
I have not had many magnums in my life. That said, the few magnums I’ve tried always seem to offer something transcendent. For example, @Rodrigo_B brought a magnum of old Williamette that I expected to hate, but was pretty good. And a few ‘value’ 750mls I bought of 2001 Gran Riserva Bodega Pecina Gran Riserva were unequivocally surpassed by a lone magnum I was able to procure. Which has me wondering… are Magnums always better? And if so… why don’t we talk more about magnums instead of 750mls? Which then had me wondering… maybe some of you ARE focusing on magnums? Now that I have a cellar full of 750mls, I have been trying to buy more magnums here and there (although they do seem to be uncommon in Burgundy as an offering?). I’m not sure where I’ll drink these, but the ones I’ve bought tend to be on the lower/middle of my purchase targets… so will be less painful to open them.

  • What % Of Wines Do You Buy/Cellar Are Magnums?
  • 0-5%
  • 5-10%
  • 10-20%
  • 20-30%
  • 30-40%
  • 40-50%
  • Over 50%
0 voters

P.S. the poll function is a little screwy or I don’t know how to use it right…

options.html: What % Of Wines Do You Buy/Cellar Are Magnums?

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked: I’ve got 60 magnums and buy several annually. They’re harder to store, take up more space and that’s probably why we have less magnums than 750

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.cooked:
I have very few. A handful of Foillards, Metras and Pedres Blanques. Typically a kind of wine that I want to enjoy with more people, including family who may not be as interested as I am.

Now that I’ve made more and more ‘dangerous’ friends from this board who I’ll meet IRL I might decide to pick up a little bit more from now on.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Mags of champs are always useful.

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked: I find having a selection of mags comes in very handy for larger dinners with friends or holiday meals. Thanksgiving comes to mind.

posts.name: Chris CrutchfieId
posts.cooked: If it’s worth buying, it’s worth buying in magnum.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked: Mags always seem to age a bit better, both longer and a finer expression of the wine than 750s. I love them.

posts.name: W_Shearer
posts.cooked: I would have more magnums if I could have them shipped to me, but more often than not they refuse.

posts.name: Richard_mao
posts.cooked: If the magnum cost less than 2 x 750ml, I would always get the magnum. But it is definitely harder finishing a magnum on your own.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: Magnums tend to stay in the cellar longer. Whilst you might assume that’s because they last longer, I think it’s mostly due to them being only opened for social gatherings, and so there’s simply less opportunity to open them.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I love the celebratory side of magnums, they are just fun and make an impression. That side, I do not buy a lot of them as the opportunity to open them, for me, is limited. My wine groups are parties of five, perfect for multiple 750s, which allows for more variety.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Probably magnums age a bit better - thermal mass, less effect of sunlight, small ratio of air to wine, etc. Larger bottlings (>1.5) have the perceived risk of poorer closer. So my bias is always hold your mags longer.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: This is more the problem - if you have wine friends you want try a few things.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I buy a good amount of magnums, still a relatively small amount of the cellar, given how many 750s there are. I think for Burgs, the aging curve becomes much more difficult to predict, because so few people open wine from magnum so it’s much more of a crapshoot as to when to open the bottles. I would say for some burgs there’s not a premium and sometimes even a discount to the 750 price, but for higher end wines like DRC and Rousseau there is often an enormous premium.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

I love the celebratory side of magnums, they are just fun and make an impression. That side, I do not buy a lot of them as the opportunity to open them, for me, is limited. My wine groups are parties of five, perfect for multiple 750s, which allows for more variety.

Maybe your groups are just lightweights, because we have no trouble with variety AND magnums!

Just kidding, my friend, and point taken. We love them at wine dinners, even of just 6 people, because it allows for following a wine over a much longer period of time without it oxidizing quickly in the glass, since you can get a fresh pour. Parities here almost alwasy start with a magnum of champagne, and on the few occasions when wine is left in the magnum at the end of an evening, I love revisiting it while cleaning up, or even the next morning.

Still, like others have said, magnums represent a small percentage of our overall cellar, though way more than 375s and probably more than a lot of people stock since we entertain with regularity. Champagne mags are the largest allocation. I think that the common belief that wine ages better in magnum is especially true for champagne. We also have a lot of Riesling magnums at least party because the shape is so fun!

posts.name: Gregory Dal Piaz
posts.cooked:
I love magnums, always have, so perhaps that gives me an advantage as I have lots of mature magnums to choose from, eliminating that age old issue that we have for the first 20 years of this hobby. So much wine and not a bottle ready to drink!

I tend to fluctuate right above 10% of the cellar. I think in a group of 4 you can start popping mags, we open 2 for 4 people. Yes you can have more variety with 750s, but it’s also nice to be able to go back and forth and follow two wines for an evening as opposed to getting a glass of each wine whenever that wine is in rotation among your guests. Non wine geeks don’t follow the rules and take bigger pours so there’s that issue as well.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked: I started off buying a fair number of mags but found I rarely open them. When I do open one for a large group, it’s always fun and I’m happy I had them, but my ultimate preference is to open 2 different wines instead of one mag.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: One aspect of a magnum in their favour, is they don’t need a ‘special’ event to open them. Just enough people, and they make such a visual impression, that they can make a gathering into an event.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Well said, Greg, and in line with my thinking. I do enjoy having a number of wines to taste, but I find more and more, my palate gets fatigued with a bunch of different wines, and at least one or two bottles get lost in the mental jumble of an evening, especially when I’m cooking/plating/serving/clearing/washing in addition to sitting and enjoying. Having a larger volume of fewer wines that I can experience over a few glasses these days gives me a better experience and ultimately leaves me with deeper understanding of the wines.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked: The reason I don’t own an Kinsman-Eades mags is that they were offered $690 vs $590 for two 750s…

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
J_Patrick_Lynch:

The reason I don’t own an Kinsman-Eades mags is that they were offered $690 vs $590 for two 750s…

I think for that kind of price differential I’d always go with the mags. For some wines the mag price is like the same as 3-4 750s.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Haha, true true! You are pro!

So in April, my family will be up in the mountains for a week, and I actually have been planning on magnums for that occasion. There will be eight of us, so plenty to go around!

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked: Frogs Leap has the best sizing pricing: 375s are half the cost of 750s and mags are double.

posts.name: @lbert Reg0
posts.cooked: Yes, great for family gatherings. I always have several with some age and ready to go.

posts.name: Brooks Haley
posts.cooked: Any winemakers on the board care to expound on why the mags can be so much higher in price? I’m guessing the bottles are just that much more expensive since they’re a novelty item?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
J_Patrick_Lynch:

Frogs Leap has the best sizing pricing: 375s are half the cost of 750s and mags are double.

I mean sometimes you can find wines cheaper in magnum than 2 750s. I think the price discrepancy for magnums on the secondary market isn’t related to the producer but just scarcity. I doubt la tache magnums ex cellar are that much more than 750 but the mags on the secondary market are 4x the price.

posts.name: John Davis
posts.cooked: To paraphrase Churchhill, “A magnum is the perfect size for two people to share over lunch, particularly if one isn’t drinking”.

posts.name: Chris Weishaar
posts.cooked:
BHaley:

I’m guessing the bottles are just that much more expensive since they’re a novelty item?

Not just the glass, but also the corks, labels, and labor involved in changing out mechanisms on the bottling line, storage space, etc. Whether or not that is all enough to directly correlate to the typical premium, I don’t know, but there is definitely more to it than we consumers tend to think about.

We’re a bit less than 5% in our cellar, the vast majority being mags, but we have a handful of double mags as well.

We were first inspired to buy mags by a couple in our blind tasting group who, on every 10th wedding anniversary, host a celebration and open nothing but magnums…Champagnes, Burgundies, Bordeaux, etc., to Port and Sauternes, all in magnum. The port is always a mag from their anniversary year- 1977. In addition to this, our group dinners are routinely 10-16 so mags make sense on those nights as well.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
When I sold bottles I always advised wineries to get more half bottles and fewer magnums.
In my own experience I find it difficult to use magnums unless there is a really big party. At a dinner party do you want a white and a red or one magnum??

posts.name: Jonathan Gr/-\|-|/-\/\/\
posts.cooked: Repour saves the day. drink what you can and either transfer the rest to a 750ml and use a repour, or use the repour in the mag bottle. i love mags

posts.name: Rob Isaacs
posts.cooked: We open mags often at dinner when there will be six to eight people. Perch Wine Co did a Amador County blend in mag for $40 that was perfect for an easy going meal with friends. Went through three of them way faster than I thought I would, will definitely buy again if they make it. Don’t get stuck thinking you should only by expensive wine in mags.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Love mags for large family gatherings. I do limit my purchasing to those which come at a modest premium over 2x750ml.

My best big bottle “premium” was a 3L of 1990 Banfi Brunello. I was just getting into wine and had no idea what I was going to do with such a big bottle, but the 750s had gone up in price at the distributor after a big WS score. My local store only had 750s from a reload at the new price, but still had a 3L for what was now effectively 3x750. Kept it for 20+ years and had it when we had 8 people around the table for my in-laws’ 50th anniversary. It was perfect. I’m looking at the empty on my home office shelf as I type.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Or two reds!

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Stopped buying mags a while back as I lost most of the opportunities to use them. Only open a couple a year, so I’m stocked for the foreseeable future.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: I love magnums but rarely open them other than Champagne. I never have enough Champagne mags.

posts.name: Brian Tuite  GOML!
posts.cooked:
Not many here, actually only 8. I never find the need to open one, nobody wants to drink the same wine all night.

*750ml - 98.1% Bottles (2,089) / Pending (71)
*375ml - 0.9% Bottles (19)
*500ml - 0.4% Bottles (8)
*1.5L - 0.4% Bottles (8)
*3.0L - 0.3% Bottles (6)

user_status.description: Wine doesn’t have to be tertiary to be enjoyable

posts.name: Richard Albert
posts.cooked: With a consumption rate of half a bottle per person over the course of an evening, a menu designed for one type of wine = perfect for four, or more.

posts.name: Terry H a r r i s
posts.cooked:
Back when I was buying more Chateauneuf du Pape I was also buying more magnums. I often would take them to our annual Christmas Eve get-together hosted by my friends. They were a huge hit, especially with the young adult children, many of whom were just getting into wine. Many of those “kids” have since moved away, those annual get-togethers are much smaller, and I have significantly reduced my CdP purchasing.

I am now down to just 2 magnums of wine. One is a 2010 CdP I plan on opening this week, as my friends come over for some racks of lamb I plan on roasting. The other is a 2007 Barolo that I will open at some point. I’ve been giving some thought to purchasing some of the PGC magnums and hope to be around still to purchase a number of 2025 vintage magnums as “birth” wines for my one and only granddaughter who was born a month ago. That will be fun!

I’ll add that the past two years I’ve purchased a number of magnums of Champagne for my daughter. She’s still not drinking (is nursing, and quit drinking when she learned she was pregnant last year), but I plan on continuing those purchases.
posts.display_username: Terry H a r r i s

posts.name: Larry G0ld3n
posts.cooked: To quote Russell From, proprietor/founder/winemaker Herman Story, “nothing parties like a magnum”. To be serious, we buy magnums whenever possible of special wine for special occasions. Special occasions are usually family events. Now all we have to do is start enjoying them.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: Although I picked up a mag as part of an auction lot last year, the last time I bought one specifically was 2016. I consume about 1 a year (usually at Thanksgiving). Doubt I’ll buy any more.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked: Mine is just below %5, but if you add in bigger bottles (3L, 3.78L, 5L) it goes a little over 5%.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Richard_Albert:

With a consumption rate of half a bottle per person over the course of an evening, a menu designed for one type of wine = perfect for four, or more.

True, but I’d generally rather have two or three different wines. Or four. In a dinner group of four.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
I find them a totally useless bottle size. You can’t open them with wine geeks because everybody will take their taste, move on to the next wine, and then half the magnum goes down the drain. You can’t open them with non-wine geeks because someone will go, “Omigod, there’s no way we’re going to drink all that wine!” and then half the magnum goes down the drain.

On very old bottles, magnums do seem to have an advantage holding on to freshness. But that’s not much of an advantage unless you have a cellar full of very old bottles. My cellar isn’t old enough for me to want my wines aging any slower than they already do.

Dare I say, there is also something inherently assholish about the format and the way some producers give their loyal customers a big F-U when they decide to bottle a particularly good or particularly rare wine only in magnum, as if to deliberately limit the number of people who can enjoy it and the occasions for doing so. What the world needs is more of those things in 375mls and 500mls.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Keith_Levenberg:

I find them a totally useless bottle size. You can’t open them with wine geeks because everybody will take their taste, move on to the next wine, and then half the magnum goes down the drain. You can’t open them with non-wine geeks because someone will go, “Omigod, there’s no way we’re going to drink all that wine!” and then half the magnum goes down the drain.

Around 2000, I was at a party with about 20 people and only one other wine geek. I brought a mature 3L of Burgess Cab. People said “Omigod, there’s no way we’re going to drink all that wine!” but they did. And a few other bottles.

My friend Jared who posts here occasionally has a big July 4th party. Mags and 3L disappear quickly. Maybe you just don’t have enough friends.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: My friends must be bigger lushes than yours - Magnums usually go fairly well although I admit I did have one last year that didn’t quite get finished. It’s the Double Magnums that I will never buy again. Just never find an opportunity to drink them.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I’ve never had a problem with mags not getting finished; usually they are gone before I’ve expected. I’d rather that happen than have 750s be gone before everyone gets to drink any.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: If you’re at an event where a magnum is easily consumed, you can, of course, accomplish the same effect with 2 x 750 bottles. Meanwhile the magnum introduces the downside of doubling down on the Russian roulette stakes of a corked bottle.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Our wine group has an annual “May Magnum Madness” dinner. One magnum per two persons. Allows more attendees and bigger pours. One of the most popular themes we do each year.

I get thru 3-5 magnum per year and have about 30 magnums. I seem to have no difficulty serving them (and finishing them) when there is a group of 6+ attendees.

I love magnums and so do most of our wine friends.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked:
Much prefer magnums over half mags…. :wink

A bachelor friend had the perfect plan of opening a champagne mag on Sundays and having a glass and a half or so every evening through the week. My wife and I decided to try that but unsurprisingly finished the mag on the monday night.

posts.name: Ethan Abraham
posts.cooked: Love them for events, Paulee-style dinners, or even just wine dinners with 8+ attendees. Yes you often end with waste but on the other hand it’s equally frustrating when it’s impossible to go back for a second pour on anything.

posts.name: Scott Watkins
posts.cooked: I learned late in life that I need more magnums and I don’t need 4 to 6 people to open one. The other night a friend of my wife came over and a mag of Champagne with a 750 of burg was perfect to linger over a 4 to 5 hour dinner for the three of us.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: 0 magnums but a bunch of half bottles.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
dbailey:

the perfect plan

Perfect maybe if you like flat Champagne?

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked: I usually have an event every year with 8 people and 3 Magnums plus a 750 or two. I have 22 magnums in my cellar and am buying a couple more this week.

posts.name: J0$hua Levin$0n
posts.cooked:
I like big bottles and I cannot lie.

I have a lot of magnums, mostly Beaujolais, Bordeaux, champagne. Riesling magnums are beautiful baseball bats and underrated too because they are typically lower in abv, but I keep drinking them. I open a few magnums a year and no one seems to complain.

I also a few 3L (really fun with champagne) and even a 6L. 3L and bigger are hard to store, and haven’t opened any yet, but they make me happy and I impulsively (compulsively?) want more. A friend used to do an annual “3l” party where everyone is supposed to bring one to attend.

Biggest issue I have is storing the large bottles, particularly off-site. With the risk of thread drift, how does one maximize space when storing large (1.5 and 3L) bottles besides OWC when available?

posts.name: jordan whitehead
posts.cooked:
Mel_Knox:

At a dinner party do you want a white and a red or one magnum??

that’s not a party. that’s just dinner

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Fewer wines also makes it easier to talk about other things, rather than the evening devolving into an analytical tasting… Which I’m absolutely fine with, but which can narrow one’s social circle

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Unfortunately the “other things” tends to be current politics. I would rather talk about wine.

posts.name: John Davis
posts.cooked: Love magnums. They’re perfect for dinner parties, especially larger ones.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
3.4% but that’s still 111 bottles and a lone jeroboam of Fourrier CSJ that is going to Paulee NYC

I agree with @Andrew_K and others: magnums of champagne are a great way to open up a gathering.

I tend to open mags in bigger groups, but am persuaded by @Sarah_Kirschbaum. I see the attraction of sharing a big special bottle with a few people to allow room for appreciation and real conversation, as opposed to hubbub and the tyranny of the boldest, biggest (flavored) wine on a crowded table.

Most of my mags are Burgundy, and I’ve seen a couple times now how things that were open for business in a 750cc were still tight and closed in a magnum format (talkin’ to you 05 Dujac and 15 Trapet)
That has me reaching for 750s while the mags sleep. When I am old and in the way, I’ll bring the thunder and wow the young-uns…

I think mags make good ticket wines: they get you in the door for a nice tasting and prime the pump for sharing. That said, I am not opening them nearly fast enough, so come on down to the ATL Berserkers!

link_counts.title: TN: 2015 Domaine Trapet Chambertin Grand Cru

posts.name: todd waldmann
posts.cooked: Love magnums. Don’t have nearly enough opportunities to open them, so my cellar is quite full of them (600+).

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
Boom! Mic drop?

No. Mag drop!

posts.name: Ethan Abraham
posts.cooked: Most dinner I go to people bring two bottles. If you have 8+ people you end up spending all your time and mental energy just figuring out when to open each, getting them around the table, etc. with 1 mag per person it gets so much easier

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
While I like them in theory they are a pain to store and I rarely have a chance to open them

I think I’m finally down to 3

posts.name: jordan whitehead
posts.cooked:
Ravinia
Fried Chicken
Champagne Magnum

Summer in Chicago

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked: 82 mags and one Marie Jeanne

posts.name: Brian Tuite  GOML!
posts.cooked:
Steve_McL:

That has me reaching for 750s while the mags sleep. When I am old and in the way, I’ll bring the thunder and wow the young-uns…

That’s what you think now. Then the time comes and you only open 1 bottle a week because you’re old now, a Mag is way too much wine and the young-uns area all drinking Pet Nat and IPAs.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
All righty then.
See you at our place at 6 and bring a glass

posts.name: H 3n ry  Br 1m 0
posts.cooked:
1.8% 1.5L
5.3% 375ml interesting enough.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: I do too, but really only buy Champagne, Cru Bojo, and German Rielsing (absolute worst for storage) mags to consume, or did, outside of mags of birthyear wines for my kids.

posts.name: Mike Vanone
posts.cooked: I’ve got one magnum of 2018 Schrader Old Sparky that I’m not sure when I’ll ever open. Not even sure why I bought it. I think I wish listed it on a whim and was then granted . It’s not a wine I’d share with my wine group and it might be a bit much for my wife and I to take in on a Saturday night. I’d probably be better off selling it.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
magnum advantage: You get to follow one wine over a period of time
magnum disadvantage: You don’t get to try another wine

magnum advantage: They age more slowly
magnum disadvantage: They age more slowly

magnum advantage: That’s a lot of wine!
magnum disadvantage: That’s a lot of wine!

I used to buy magnums (and sometimes larger) for my national sales meetings; with 12 - 25 people it’s only one or two good glasses per person. I’ve got a few strays left in my basement from those days and by now they all have real bottle age. But I want to taste every wine ever made and magnums keep me from getting closer to my goal unless there is a crowd of people. My family is not interested in wine so even when I’ve got a crowd, it still means I’ll probably only have one wine to drink at the event and maybe through the next day as well.

posts.name: Bob Hoelting
posts.cooked: Less than 1% Magnum in my 1000 bottle collection. The rest are Trojan

posts.name: jordan whitehead
posts.cooked: pairing down all large format. Storage a major issues. After breaking a 3 liter and a 6 liter. Never again going that large

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: Thank you for all the responses. What I find very interesting is people don’t see the squeeze in pursuing mags despite their obviously showing better. I find that interesting because many of us chase that extra degree of complexity and people are willing to pay a lot for that degree in a 750ml, but it doesn’t extend to mags for the reasons highlighted in the thread above.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Shan_A:

Thank you for all the responses. What I find very interesting is people don’t see the squeeze in pursuing mags despite their obviously showing better. I find that interesting because many of us chase that extra degree of complexity and people are willing to pay a lot for that degree in a 750ml, but it doesn’t extend to mags for the reasons highlighted in the thread above.

I do for champagne, but for burgs, it adds an extra degree of complexity figuring out when they’re actually ready to drink in mag. It would be easy to open them prematurely and they would be showing worse, not better. If we’re saying burgs show best 20-25 years from vintage date that might be 30-35 for mags; compounded with the difficulty in storage for some people and significantly increased costs and I think it’s obvious why people aren’t dedicating a ton of their wine budget to them. I think I have maybe 10-20 burgundy mags.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: It’s not so hard to plan a larger party for a really large format bottle. If that’s the thematic center, no one is concerned about variety - the specific wine is what the event is about. We have a 12L of a 2009 Barolo for my stepson’s birthyear. I imagine we’ll invite everyone we know.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
John_Morris:

Best part of the poll – the 1% who voted for the question:

image796×87 7.87 KB

For some folks, the question IS the answer!

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked: In the early 80s I lived in a small house in rural Virginia. I crammed in 20+ people and opened a Melchior red Bordeaux. With wooden case, it weighed 85 pounds. I spent the entire evening pouring for people, didn’t get a sip or a morsel of food until 2+ hours after we started. One of the great evenings of my life.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: A syphon is a good solution for serving these very large bottles without disruption to the sediment, but at the end of harvest last year I organized a set up with what was in retrospect an excessive differential, with the wine coming out with such velocity that several of my harvesters were doused with 1964 Clos l’Oratoire… lesson learned

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

A syphon is a good solution for serving these very large bottles without disruption to the sediment, but at the end of harvest last year I organized a set up with what was in retrospect an excessive differential, with the wine coming out with such velocity that several of my harvesters were doused with 1964 Clos l’Oratoire… lesson learned

I hate when that happens!

posts.name: Bill Mauger
posts.cooked: On my first visit to OR I remarked to a very large contributor here that I regretted not purchasing more “big bottles”. I have many of his 1.5’s and larger in the cellar now! He gifted us a 1.5L that had yet to be labeled that we enjoyed in the house we rented that trip with two other couples. I seem to have remedied this problem over the last 10 years. Love to pull 1.5’s out for dinner parties, holidays and going to our racquets club for summer cookouts. They have a fun aspect to many - they don’t all have to be great producers. For example, show up at the club with a 3.0L of rose and just pass around - fun! Fun to have the occasional 5.0L for a special occasion like a graduation party. Love big bottles!

posts.name: Loren Supp
posts.cooked:
Over the past year, I’ve noticed an uptick in Magnum consumption - often with older bottles brought by friends. We also did a one-off wine night with a “Magnum” theme (in both attire and bottle format) last July. It was a hit.

IMG_39455712×4284 3.57 MB

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
It is so damned hard to clean clothing that’s been doused in Chateauneuf.
Next time use German Spatburgunder, or maybe even white wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Wrong oratory! This was the Saint-Émilion!

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: I have one problem with magnums. If I’m gonna buy one, it’s gonna be a great bottle of wine. I’m only gonna open a bottle like that with Wine friends who will appreciate it. There in lies the problem. Like me, all those friends have more wine than they could possibly consume. When I drink with them, they are all bringing wine no matter what anyone says. So there’s gonna be a lot of wine there and more wine than people can drink so it’s tough to find occasions to bring them to unless there are some non-wine enthusiasts coming thereby violating who I want to drink it with

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: I can certainly see that. On the flipside, we often buy magnums of easier drinking, highly useful, wines like cru Beaujolais and 1er Chablis precisely because they are the perfect tool for the job at gatherings with non-geek friends and family.

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: And I can see that as well. I just don’t have those friends, coworkers and family that would benefit from that.

posts.name: Howard Schultz
posts.cooked:
We too usually have the large format bottles with wine friends. When do ope the larger formats warn them no need to bring wine as usual unless it’s a larger gathering.
We have taken magnums on get away weekends but not often.
Not sure how many more years I will buy larger formats but for now I will continue to be a buyer.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

I love the celebratory side of magnums, they are just fun and make an impression. That side, I do not buy a lot of them as the opportunity to open them, for me, is limited. My wine groups are parties of five, perfect for multiple 750s, which allows for more variety.

I agree, when you’re doing a wine tasting dinner with your friends, it almost always makes more sense to have more 750s than fewer 1.5s.

For that reason, I’ve come to realize that magnums usually work for me for more relaxed social gatherings or for a dinner out with a non-geek couple where I can just bring one along (and don’t have to carry multiple bottles or a wine tote), and so I’m more likely to buy good mid priced magnums that have appeal beyond wine geeks, and usually buy my most premium wines in 750.

The mags of wines that deserve attention from wine folks, they just tend to sit waiting for the right occasion that never comes along. Of course, Sideways and all, someday I’ll open them and that will be the occasion, but I don’t really want to buy and to fill my storage with very many for that reason.

[I see after posting that @Sarah_Kirschbaum and @David_Baum expressed similar thoughts.]

posts.name: Terry H a r r i s
posts.cooked:
Terry_H_a_r_r_i_s:

Back when I was buying more Chateauneuf du Pape I was also buying more magnums. I often would take them to our annual Christmas Eve get-together hosted by my friends. They were a huge hit, especially with the young adult children, many of whom were just getting into wine. Many of those “kids” have since moved away, those annual get-togethers are much smaller, and I have significantly reduced my CdP purchasing.

I am now down to just 2 magnums of wine. One is a 2010 CdP I plan on opening this week, as my friends come over for some racks of lamb I plan on roasting. The other is a 2007 Barolo that I will open at some point. I’ve been giving some thought to purchasing some of the PGC magnums and hope to be around still to purchase a number of 2025 vintage magnums as “birth” wines for my one and only granddaughter who was born a month ago. That will be fun!

I’ll add that the past two years I’ve purchased a number of magnums of Champagne for my daughter. She’s still not drinking (is nursing, and quit drinking when she learned she was pregnant last year), but I plan on continuing those purchases.

This magnum (back in March) was both a hit with the dinner guests and paired perfectly with the lamb.

IMG_17691920×1440 285 KB
posts.display_username: Terry H a r r i s

reply_to_user.name: Terry H a r r i s

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
I am hosting a birthday dinner for a close friend in late July and at present the head count is 13. So a good opportunity to open mags, especially because while everyone coming likes wine, there are no geeks expected (well, besides me and I’ll be performing Sarah’s list of hosting chores). Crab (and summer produce) will be the main course so I am planning on starting with a mag of 2012 Roederer rose and then a mag of 2018 Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru “Clos de la Mouchère”, both of which I also have in 750 should we need a third bottle. Don’t have any dessert wine in mags but a 750 of something for which I also own a 375 seems appropriate.

Sorry, grandniece, for opening your puligny.

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked: We are doing a luncheon for 7 on Sunday. We will have 2 Magnums, 2010 Tempier Bandol and Roederer 242. I will mix in a 2021 Tempier Blanc and a couple of Roses in 750s.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I wish I had fewer magnums. Great format for aging, but we do wine dinners and try and taste as many wines as possible. Magnums don’t fit.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Opposite end of the spectrum for me - I have almost zero interest in tasting a bunch of wines at a dinner anymore. A couple of ounces of 20 wines is just not enjoyable. I want a decent pour of every wine I drink, and I want to spend time with it. Depending on the number of diners, magnums can be ideal. I still drink enough different wines over the course of the year to feel my experience is broad. Even for educational purposes, my understanding the wines I drink at a 10+ bottle event is a fraction of my understanding of wines I can spend time with. Perhaps it’s a limitation of my palate. Or perhaps the very notion of tasting, vs drinking, simply isn’t my thing anymore.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked: Mark, Mark, Mark…we’re here for you man.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Sarah_Kirschbaum:

Opposite end of the spectrum for me - I have almost zero interest in tasting a bunch of wines at a dinner anymore. A couple of ounces of 20 wines is just not enjoyable. I want a decent pour of every wine I drink, and I want to spend time with it. Depending on the number of diners, magnums can be ideal. I still drink enough different wines over the course of the year to feel my experience is broad. Even for educational purposes, my understanding the wines I drink at a 10+ bottle event is a fraction of my understanding of wines I can spend time with. Perhaps it’s a limitation of my palate. Or perhaps the very notion of tasting, vs drinking, simply isn’t my thing anymore.

Sarah, I agree with you in principle, but most of our dinners are between six and eight people, and there is ample time to taste and watch the evolution.

posts.name: Richard Albert
posts.cooked:
I continue to assume average consumption of alcohol for the general population over the course of an evening is half a bottle per person, mags are perfect for as few as four people.
No special occasion required, IMO.
I am a mag supporter, especially for the pleasure of serving older wines.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:
Sarah_Kirschbaum:

Opposite end of the spectrum for me - I have almost zero interest in tasting a bunch of wines at a dinner anymore. A couple of ounces of 20 wines is just not enjoyable. I want a decent pour of every wine I drink, and I want to spend time with it. Depending on the number of diners, magnums can be ideal. I still drink enough different wines over the course of the year to feel my experience is broad. Even for educational purposes, my understanding the wines I drink at a 10+ bottle event is a fraction of my understanding of wines I can spend time with. Perhaps it’s a limitation of my palate. Or perhaps the very notion of tasting, vs drinking, simply isn’t my thing anymore.

Sarah, I agree with you in principle, but most of our dinners are between six and eight people, and there is ample time to taste and watch the evolution.

It still doesn’t really work for me. 6 is the very most I want on a bottle, and even that isn’t satisfying to me. A 4oz pour is hard to nurse over 2 hours or more, i don’t like tiny sips, and as it gets less and less, it oxidizes more and more quickly, without the ability to top it up. You can end up with lots of glasses going, and my palate doesn’t easily switch back and forth again and again among more than 3 wines tops. In the end, a lot of bottles at one sitting just doesn’t appeal to me on any level anymore. I am patient. I’d rather slowly enjoy 5 to 6 wines tops on a sitting with a group, and then do it again down the road sometime. Any more than that is akin to a receiving line for me.
